title: Psalms 101
subtitle: I Will Walk with a Blameless Heart

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — David Captures Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–4 — “I Will Conduct the Affairs of My House with a Blameless Heart”

heb.paragraph: "I will be careful to lead a blameless (tāmîm) life. I will conduct the affairs of my house with a blameless heart" (v.2). A royal psalm of personal integrity. David’s leadership pledge: no vile thing before my eyes (v.3), no perverse heart near me (v.4). The king’s household reflects the king’s character.

notes.note: "I will sing of your love and justice. I will be careful to lead a blameless life." Love and justice as the king’s song; blamelessness as the king’s conduct.
"I will not look with approval on anything that is vile. I will have nothing to do with what is evil." The king’s eyes and associations are curated. What enters the palace shapes the kingdom.

refs.note: David administering justice and righteousness fulfills the psalm's royal ethics.
Love and faithfulness keeping a king safe echoes the psalm's values.
Qualifications for overseers parallel the psalm's standards for royal household.

hist.context: Psalm 101 is a royal oath of integrity attributed to David. The king commits to blameless conduct in his palace, refusing to look at worthless things or tolerate deceit. This represents the ideal monarch's ethical program — a self-imposed standard of righteousness that would govern appointments and administration.

sections.header: Verses 5–8 — “Every Morning I Will Silence the Wicked”

heb.paragraph: "Every morning (babbōqer) I will put to silence all the wicked in the land" (v.8). The king’s daily judicial duty: remove evil. Morning by morning, justice is administered. The faithful of the land will be David’s companions (v.6). Deceivers and liars will not stand in his presence (v.7). Integrity is the admission policy for the royal court.

notes.note: "My eyes will be on the faithful in the land. Whoever has a blameless walk will serve me." The king surrounds himself with integrity. Character, not connections, determines proximity.
"Every morning I will put to silence all the wicked." Daily justice. Not sporadic but systematic. The morning routine includes purging evil from the city of the LORD.

refs.note: The qualifications for dwelling on God's holy hill parallel this psalm's standards.
Removing the wicked from the king's presence establishes the throne in righteousness.
Nothing impure entering the holy city fulfills the psalm's exclusion of the wicked.

hist.context: The daily purging of 'all the wicked from the land' (v.8) describes ongoing vigilance rather than a single act. The 'city of the LORD' (v.8) is Jerusalem, and the king takes responsibility for its moral character. This psalm shaped later expectations for messianic rule characterized by justice and purity.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in I Will Walk with a Blameless Heart.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: God is a key figure in I Will Walk with a Blameless Heart.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Solomon
ppl.text: Solomon is a key figure in I Will Walk with a Blameless Heart.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe perverse of heart shall be far from me; I will have nothing to do with what is evil.ESVA perverse heart shall be far from me; I will know nothing of evil.NIVMy eyes will be on the faithful in the land, that they may dwell with me; the one whose walk is blameless will minister to me.ESVI will look with favor on the faithful in the land, that they may dwell with me; he who walks in the way that is blameless shall minister to me.

rec.text: ""I will sing of your love and justice. I will be careful to lead a blameless life." Love and justice as the king’s song; blamelessness as the king’s conduct."

rows.label: Morning Justice — 101:5–8
rows.text: "Every MORNING I will silence the wicked." Faithful companions. Daily purging.

hebtext.note: "I will be careful to lead a blameless (tāmîm) life. I will conduct the affairs of my house with a blameless heart" (v.2). A royal psalm of personal integrity. David’s leadership pledge: no vile thing before my eyes (v.3), no perverse heart near me (v.4). The king’s household reflects the king’s character.

"Every morning (babbōqer) I will put to silence all the wicked in the land" (v.8). The king’s daily judicial duty: remove evil. Morning by morning, justice is administered. The faithful of the land will be David’s companions (v.6). Deceivers and liars will not stand in his presence (v.7). Integrity is the admission policy for the royal court.

themes.note: Holiness and Justice peak: the king’s personal integrity and daily justice administration. The royal character psalm.
